speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 29th of September, 1995. The survivor is Stephen Muller. The interviewer is Shirley Eisenberg. We are in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia, and the interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: 29, 1995. The survivor is Stephen Muller, S-T-E-P-H-E-N. Muller is M-U- double L-E-R. The interviewer is Shirley Eisenberg, S-H-I-R-L-E-Y E-I-S-E-N-B-E-R-G. It's in Sydney, Australia, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen Muller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-P-H-E-N M-U- double L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, I was called Stefan, S-T-E-F-A-N, Muller but with an umlaut on the u, the two dots on the u, which is a German version of-- of the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22Nd of May, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little village called Bochnia in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your family home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's rather vague in my mind, but I do remember several things about it. It was-- I remember the village itself, and that was also reinforced when I came to see it again later on in my life. It was a sort of small-- smallish, rural type of Polish-- typical Polish town. We had a fairly large home, I think, with a very large kitchen. The reason why I remember the kitchen is that I ran along the polished floor, not too dissimilar to what we have here in this house, as a young child and I slipped. And I bashed myself in the corner of a chair, just above my right eye. And if I had-- if I had done that just a little bit below, I would be now speaking with one blind eye. So that's a memory-- the first traumatic memory that I have. And sort of the surroundings of the place that I lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other rooms of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one bedroom because I was sick one day. And in those days, the doctor used to come, and was, of course, before the antibiotic era, or before any type of medication, really. And they used to put these very hot, glass-- like glasses, on top-- on your back. And that was supposed to draw out the inflammation from your lungs. And you can imagine for a three-year-old, I think I was at that stage, to have these things applied to your back was also very traumatic experience. And therefore, that's-- I remember the bedroom, lying on the bed, and the doctor actually heating these they were called [POLISH] in Polish, and putting them on your back. And the suction, because the air is sucked out of it, and it sort of sticks to your skin. And it makes red circles around it. Actually, I saw a patient the other day with a very similar thing. He was Chinese. So it's still obviously used amongst some ethnic people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else did you live with in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my parents. I was the only child. I still am. And what we used to have in those days is a wet nurse, is a woman who actually breastfed me. It wasn't unusual for mothers of children to actually go to work, and my mother did work at that stage. And I was looked after by a-- by a Polish woman who was constantly-- well, she wasn't pregnant. As long as you-- you were-- you were breastfeeding, the milk supply used to come. So I remember being put on-- well, I can't remember being put on the breast, but I remember her very well. And also, I think I was quite-- it was not unusual to be breastfed for quite a long time. where as now it's only, you know, maybe six months. It wasn't unusual for a child to be breastfed until about, what, three years of age, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special toys as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any toys whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any special food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the sort of typical kosher food that people used to eat. But I don't remember-- I don't really remember the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any friends that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I can't-- I cannot recall any of those. There's certain things I recall. One thing which stuck in my mind, which my mother said, that one of her friends had a stillborn child. And the reason why the-- why the child was stillborn was because they lived across the street from a church. And the woman used to, not infrequently, look out of the window at the statue of Jesus Christ who was, obviously, dead on the cross. And this is [INAUDIBLE] that she had a stillborn child. And she believed in that sort of folk-- folklore, even to-- when she migrated to Australia, she still believed in lots of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were religious. Although my grandparents were more religious than-- than my parents were. My-- especially my father was more-- what should I say-- revolutionary. It was difficult not to be religious in a small community like that. I think he was a bit more avant garde than the average.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Shabbat at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember that far back. Or not-- I can't remember a particular Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special foods? Any other celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember any of those. I suppose I can't remember the sort of happy events. I can only remember the traumatic events.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other traumatic events that you do remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I try and think back, the only thing that-- were the two that I described. I think I probably-- my father was away a lot. I think he was-- well, I do know he was in the Polish army. So he-- he was away. I didn't have much contact with him. I don't remember my father in the early stages. And my mother also used to go to work, so I had-- I had very little contact with them. So most of my sort of day to day contacts were with my nurse, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working. They had a hardware store, and she was working in the hardware store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you were obliged to leave the family home in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't remember the exact time, but I do remember that we were taken from one particular part of the-- this village and, all of a sudden, we were in a different area, which seemed like a long way away because, obviously, I was very small. And the-- we were all herded into one small room. So it was before I probably had my own room and had the space. I suddenly found myself living with my parents in this one-- one particular room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the journey, actually traveling across there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the journey because I don't think it was terribly far. It was obviously, like, perhaps from one-- well, they weren't suburbs in those days, in that particular place. But I imagine it wouldn't be very far. It'd be from, like, one suburb to the next. Like, the equivalent of a suburb in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it that you and your family were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a part of Bochnia which was segregated into a ghetto. What apparently happened was that they decided to, let's say, take a section of the town, put up some wire-- electric wire fences around it. I suppose they took an area which, I suppose, had a lot of Jewish people in it, because they would have had to displace the other people who were living in it. And put people in there. And they had to fend for themselves, where they-- where they actually lived. I think that what happened was that there was a sort of a joint committee. I mean, they started getting themselves organized, of course, in this ghetto. And there were rooms allotted to various people, who'd try and fit them in. The reason why that is because people kept coming from other towns. I do remember people from other places coming into the ghetto. Like, there's one particular person that I do remember very well was a young girl who must have been, I suppose, [SIGHS] about 16 or 17. And she actually was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an orphan, so her parents were killed. And for some reason, she came to our ghetto. She was transferred to our ghetto, and she came to live with us. And she stayed with us for a while and then she was-- she was taken somewhere. But I do remember her staying with us. I remember her well, actually, because she was-- she was very tall, I thought, for a small child. And I would imagine she was about 16 years of age. And the other people that came to live with us were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. But this-- this particular couple I remember well because subsequently we had a very long-lasting relationship with them, as far as even coming all the way to Australia. And they came from a town called Lodz. And they were Mr. and Mrs. Bilfelb. And they were displaced from Lodz and were put into our ghetto. And they actually-- this is how we met them, because they were allotted into the space where we were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: The space that you were allotted, can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a-- maybe two-story building, and we had a room upstairs somewhere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a kitchen downstairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reason why I remember the kitchen downstairs was it was the hiding place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was-- it had a cellar, which the only way you could access it was through the kitchen, under the stove, which had a false floor. Actually, the stove-- the old-fashioned type of stove, was lifted out and people went-- the floor-boards were removed and people hid in this cellar under the stove, or the entrance, which was under the stove.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would hide under the stove?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were quite a few, so it must have been a sort of communal hiding place for particular people in this particular house that we were in, or this particular two-story place, because it was very crowded. And it was very-- it was-- it happened very-- at odd times and very frequently, it seemed to me. Because all of a sudden, I was bundled out and told not to cry or utter a sound, whisked down into the kitchen, and hidden underneath. And my mother used to keep a little bit of chocolate, so she would bribe me with that, and I would eat that and not cry. And there were other little children there, too, and no one was allowed to cry. And the reason-- I remember distinctly-- was that the cry of a child used to give the hiding place away. And she used to tell me this, that-- that, you know, her friends whose child had cried, the Germans had come and picked them up and taken them away to the concentration camps. So I was not allowed to utter a sound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very afraid, yes. I think fear was my-- my sort first experience of life. And I assumed for many years that fear was a normal-- a normal state. And the sort of environment that we lived in I imagined was the whole world. Because, of course, a child at that age doesn't understand that there's something beyond the area where they're actually living. And I remember that I thought this was how the whole world existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this hiding space, how many people would have been down there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were quite a lot because you weren't-- you had-- virtually had to stand. Sort of-- so there must have been quite a few. It seemed to me like a large space, but very, very little room in between.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think more than that. I would think maybe about 20 or 30 people used to hide in there. Of various ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would've been the longest period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we would stay there all night. My parent-- my father told me that what happened was that, he had some sort of contacts with people on the outside of the ghetto, and they knew approximately when the pogrom-- I suppose that's the word I should use-- was going to happen. And he had some sort of forewarning, because he always seemed to know when to hide and rush us down there, because apparently they just came in indiscriminately and would walk down the street, or walk into a house, and whoever was there was taken. They had a quota system of taking x number of Jews or people, whatever it was. And they didn't discriminate in the sense that they didn't have to look too hard to find them. So if you had some forewarning you were able to, perhaps, hide and avoid the inevitable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other children or other people who were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, as I said I was told by my mother and warned about uttering a sound. And as a form of-- how should I put it-- to scare me she told me the stories of other children that had been taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sleeping quarters-- can you describe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a-- just a small bed next to my parents', in one small room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What possessions did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think we had very-- I'm not sure. I don't think we had very much. It's just the sort of clothes that we had and a few cooking utensils. And very little food, actually. I think the food-- the Poles used to throw the-- the chickens over the electric wires into the ghetto. I remember that. That's how some people used to get some of their food. Or some of their-- because-- and I remember also that the Red Cross used to send parcels. And occasionally, a Red Cross parcel would arrive, and it had all sorts of goodies like I'd never seen before, like biscuits and chocolates and things. So that was-- but that was very rare. I remember most of the time that my mother used to have a chicken and she used to burn it over the fire, sort of, to get rid of all the feathers before she could cook it. And I think that's how they got the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not very much that I can remember. Nothing that-- that was-- that a child likes, anyway. Nothing special that was-- that was something that I could remember. Because I was always bribed with-- with chocolate to keep quiet if they had some. So I remember it must have been a very big treat to get a piece of chocolate, because I'd never had any of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were other children there, but I don't remember playing with any of them. I think we were just-- I just had to sit in-- in-- inside and not even go outside. Because my mother-- my parents were very worried about me going out on the street as just a small child. I only went-- anywhere where I went, I went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what activities you did through the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there's no memory of that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember bathing or showering or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have a shower. I think it was-- it was sort of like a basin. Or, no, it was-- no, I do remember now, actually. It was a big tub in the middle of the kitchen that they used to pour the water in. I think everybody had a sort of a bath like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hot water or cold water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it-- I think it was hot, so they must have had some form of electricity, or some form of heat. No-- that's right. She used to heat it on this particular stove. That's right. She used to heat it on there, in this big-- well, now you've actually reminded me of it. I remember the big kettles, those big huge kettles that they used to boil water in. And that was boiled on this wooden stove, which was then poured into the-- into this large basin on the floor. And that's how we bathed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the toilet facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember it. I don't know whether we have a bathroom or not. I cannot remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go outside at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I very seldom went outside. I can't remember ever being allowed out to play. I was-- I spent most of my time indoors. And that was over a period-- it must have been in the summer as well, because we were there for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get very hot or very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very cold, yes. I don't remember-- it wasn't very hot, but it was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was no heating. There was no heating. So it was just a question of-- of sort of huddling together. And the clothes I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any songs that your mother or father sand to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no songs. But my mother tried to teach me because there was no school. So I think she tried to teach me to read and write. Because she was-- she was-- she went to business college, so she had some form of sort of training. Not as a teacher, but she had formal training, as such. So she tried to teach me how to read and write in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember doing any writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember writing, yes. But I don't remember-- and I didn't have any books. And there were no books. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you draw any pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I drew pictures. Houses, mostly. And therefore, they thought that when I would grow up, I would become an engineer-- an architect, actually, because I drew lots of houses, for some reason. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: For four years. From 1939, so it must have-- I think it was the 1st of September. I just read the other day that the war-- that Hitler came to Poland from the 1st of September, 1939 til 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the conversations of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember some. The thing that remind-- that I remember very distinctly was the main topic of conversation, [DRINKS] apart from survival, was the Messiah. And even as a young child at that age, I remember that they believed that the Messiah was miraculously going to come out of the heavens, whisk them out of this little ghetto, and-- and free them. And everybody believed this. And this was sort of instilled in me, and I thought about-- and I-- I imagined what this Messiah was going to be like. I remember the Messiah very well-- I suppose this was not unusual for people to believe in such a thing-- that it would-- he would actually come and rescue you. Because there was no-- the feeling was that there was no way of being rescued. There was one time, actually where-- my father recounted, and I remember this well, because he said that they came and they said that if you have any relatives outside of Poland, they would be-- we would be deported to that country.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother had a sister in America. And they said-- they took quite a few people and said, you will now be deported to-- deported, I suppose we would all like to be deported from there-- to America. And we were taken from this ghetto in Bochnia to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were standing, and the part that I remember was there were these huge logs standing outside-- out at the back of this huge building, which I suppose must have been some sort of official building. And we were standing there for hours in the cold waiting to be admitted to this-- to this place, which was going to be a-- a focus of-- of being taken out from there, by train I assume. And after many hours of standing out in the cold with my parents on these huge logs-- which obviously weren't as big, but to me, they seemed huge-- we were taken into this huge hole-- again, it probably wasn't as huge, but again, then, because I'm small-- I was small. And we wait-- we stayed there for quite a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nothing happened. We were taken back to the ghetto. So it was-- I'm not quite sure why they did this, but it is something that I remember well because my parents had thought, this is going to be it, you know? We are going to be rescued. Do you remember when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it happened about-- maybe about two years after we were in the ghetto. It was quite a while after we actually went there. But it was-- it was-- I can't understand what the reason was. I mean, to go to the extreme of taking all these people-- there must have been quite a few people-- from one-- one part of Poland to another, herding them there, and saying, well, now, if you've got relatives in somewhere else, you're going to go there. I think you had to pay something, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then say, oh no, you know? That's not all. Back to the ghetto you go. But that I remember very well because it was-- it was something that we all sort of looked forward to but it didn't eventuate. Do you remember going back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember going back to-- back to where we started from.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my feeling throughout all this was always a fear, you see? I had the fear of uniforms, because the German army always were very well dressed with these boots, always shiny, and beautiful uniforms with caps, you see? And that was-- that was something that I feared all my life, even to the-- to a very late age. When I was in Australia, I couldn't really relate to anybody in uniform. It used to always conjure up this picture in me. And this was the fear of-- of-- of a small child, seeing someone in uniform, in authority. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've swerved off the topic somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any punishment in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. We used to hear that people were actually flogged. And there was a square there. And-- I suppose I used to listen to-- to people talking at night, and during the day-- mostly at night, actually-- about what was-- what was happening. And I remember that they said that quite a few people were either flogged or shot in the square of the-- of this village. The village always used to have a main square, like the-- like the village square, I suppose, which it was. I actually went to that square many years later, when I went back to try and find the house where I lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends of your age in ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one family who had a child about the same age as-- as me. I think his name was Adam. And they were roughly about my age, also a one-child family-- one son. And I think they lived in the-- very nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember playing with him, but I remember escaping with him. But not playing with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Jewish observance in the ghetto? Did anyone light candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They did, I-- I remember they did. They tried, anyway. And they did light Friday night candles. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I think they prayed, sort of surreptitiously, in a way. They hid when they were praying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any happy times in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no presents. I can't remember any birthdays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than Adam, were there any other friends? Any young children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only this 16-year-old girl who lived with us. But nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your hope for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure whether a child has-- thinks that way. I don't know what a child thinks at that age. But I certainly didn't. I can't remember having any hope, as such. I'm not sure whether this is a normal reaction of any child to any situation, but I think-- there was no joy in any of this. I can't remember any happy times. Now, if you ask a child, does he remember-- does he or she remember certain things or certain-- I think they do remember some of the good times. But I honestly can't remember anything apart from the-- the only things that-- as you're asking me, do I remember anything that was happy, I can't. And the only things I can remember is the traumatic things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father and your mother, do you remember their activities throughout the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they-- they used to take them out, every day, to work outside of the ghetto. There was a procession of people that were taken out every day, every morning, and came back at night. And I think I must have stayed with some of the older-- I think they were the younger people that went out. The older people stayed inside. But The younger people who could work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were taken out. There was also a sort of a-- like a committee of-- of Jews who actually ran the ghetto on a volu-- obviously on a voluntary basis, who were sort of in charge of things. And they had some sort of liaison with-- with the authorities. But I can't remember what else. But mostly, I remember my parents walking out every day to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents involved in the Jewish council?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my father was on the Jewish council because he seemed to have more information than-- now that I understand what happened-- he seemed to have more information about what was happening than I would imagine anybody else would have. And that may have-- may have made a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now, Stephen.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape one of the interview with Stephen Muller. It's the 29th of September, 1995 and we are in Sydney, Australia.
